description: Golden labels for 50 sampled per-extraction dedup cases (25 observations, 25 strategy_points). Labels use current D/M/K scheme. Strategy points only have D/K (no merge).

label_schema.D: DISCARD — duplicate of an existing candidate, bump count
label_schema.M: MERGE — merge with an existing candidate (observations only)
label_schema.K: KEEP — distinct, keep as a new entry

labels.notes: Strategic lesson same as C2 (persist with same target). 2 vs 3 saves is degree not kind.
Proactive wolf tactic of framing correct votes as suspicious — distinct from defensive deflection.
Bussing strategy — wolf votes with majority to eliminate partner. All candidates show opposite pattern.
Confirmed non-wolf navigating post-mislynch — distinct from C1's questioning approach.
Same as C2: own the mistake, pivot to analysis.
Same as C1: target vocal organizer Night 1.
Vote manipulation to create deadlock — distinct from bussing in C1.
Same as C1: attack timing of Investigator reveal.
Day 1 healer should avoid being vocal — distinct early-game survival advice.
Same as C1: partner doomed, vote with majority.
Night 1 investigate quiet/low-substance player — distinct from mid-game candidates.
2v1 endgame wolf 'too coordinated' tactic is distinct. LLM mislabeled as merge.
Same endgame voting-record pattern as C2, adds specific evidence detail.
Wolves discrediting dead Investigator — enriches C1's general pattern.
Wolf questioning healer saves — specific instance of C4's deflection pattern.
Same credibility-destroyed Investigator pattern as C5.
Late bussing detection — distinct heuristic from C2's partner-protection detection. LLM mislabeled as merge.
C2 already generalizes this pattern well. Borderline D/M.
Same find-and-die pattern as C2.
Exact same groupthink tactic as C5. Clear discard.
Same as C1: investigate dissenting voter. LLM mislabeled as replace.
Same as C1: treat posthumous Investigator accusation as ground truth.
Aggressive player might be frustrated Investigator — distinct from C2's general anti-consensus advice. LLM mislabeled as replace.
New entry is better written (emotional framing + evidence). Discard with rewrite.
Behavioral test + meta-accusation of coordinated dismissal — distinct from C4's passive guidance.
Same as C1: use wolf targeting as credibility evidence.
C2 already mentions deadlock scenario. Same reveal-under-pressure advice.
Early game, challenge accuser pressuring quiet players — distinct redirect strategy from C2. LLM mislabeled as replace.
Covers both emotional framing and evidence. Discard with rewrite of C1.
Success vs failure of same strategy (protect vocal player Night 1). Opposite outcomes teach risk/reward.
Same bandwagon-no-evidence pattern as C1. Confusing writing made it seem distinct. LLM mislabeled as keep.
Coordinated two-wolf counter-vote is distinct from lone dissenting vote in C1.
Same dissenting-vote approach, but succeeds (avoids suspicion) vs C1 where it fails. Different outcome = different lesson.
Wolves specifically identify and kill the Healer — distinct from pivoting to generic villager.
Timing attack on Investigator reveal fails (4 players, strong credibility) vs C2 where it succeeds (1v1v1). Context determines outcome.
Same 'too obvious' defense, same outcome. Phase difference doesn't change lesson. LLM mislabeled as keep.
Caught by discussion/voting contradiction + partner protest vote backfires — distinct trigger and compound dynamic.
Conflicting advice with C1 (vote third target vs vote with majority). Both valid — gives agent tactical flexibility.
3-player endgame swing vote evaluating Investigator claim — completely different from C1.
After wolf eliminated, focus on dissenters — distinct from C1's villager-eliminated trigger.
Detecting deflection from mistake analysis as wolf signal — distinct from C3 and C4.
Weaponize post-mislynch paranoia against accuser — distinct from C2's timing attack and C5's passive exploitation.
Same as C2/C3: protect vocal player Night 1. LLM mislabeled as keep.
Opposite defensive strategies: frame bad vote as deliberate test vs admit mistake (C1). Both valid options.
Hero status enables aggressive accusation — opposite of C1's subtle low-profile approach. Different public standing = different tactic.
